title: Multi-colour suminagashi
subtitle: Building concentric ring patterns with alternating sumi and dispersing ink
excerpt: Suminagashi, ink floating, is a Japanese marbling tradition in which drops of ink and dispersant are applied to plain water in alternating layers, each drop pushing the previous outward into expanding concentric rings. Using multiple colours produces complex layered patterns that shift from the centre out. Unlike Western carrageenan-bath marbling, suminagashi uses no size: the inks float on pure water.
sourceNotes: Suminagashi is a Japanese tradition documented from the Heian period (794-1185). The technique is described in various pre-modern Japanese craft records. English-language documentation appears in twentieth-century craft publications. The technique itself, alternating drops of sumi ink and pine resin dispersant on plain water, is unchanged from its historical form.

glossaryTerms.definition: Literally 'ink floating' in Japanese. A marbling technique in which drops of sumi ink and a dispersing agent are applied alternately to plain water, each drop pushing the previous outward. The concentric rings formed are transferred to paper by laying the sheet on the surface.

In suminagashi, the second ink in the alternating pair, a clear or lightly coloured ink containing a surfactant (traditionally pine resin) that lowers surface tension and pushes the sumi ink outward when dropped in the centre of a ring.

The traditional way of moving the suminagashi pattern before printing: breathing gently across the surface to cause gentle, organic movement in the rings. This produces the characteristic flowing, naturalistic patterns of suminagashi rather than the geometric combed patterns of Western marbling.

content.text: works on a different principle from Western marbling. There is no thickened size: the inks float on plain water, held at the surface by the ink's own surface tension and pushed outward by the
dropped alternately with the sumi. The patterns it produces, nested rings that shift in colour from the centre out, are very different in character from Western combed marbling.

items.name: Suminagashi ink set, at minimum, black sumi and one dispersant-loaded ink per colour
items.substitutions: Sumi ink (black) paired with a drop of pure ox-gall as dispersant (less traditional but workable)

items.name: Cartridge paper, 130 gsm, mordanted with potash alum, optional for suminagashi but improves colour adhesion on sized cartridge
items.substitutions: Unmordanted thin printer paper; washi absorbs suminagashi directly without mordanting

content.text: Fill the tray with clean water. The water must be still. Work in a draught-free space.
Touch the tip of the sumi ink brush or dropper to the exact centre of the water surface. The sumi should spread into a disc 2 to 4 cm across.
Drop the dispersant ink precisely in the centre of the sumi disc. The dispersant lowers surface tension at that point, pushing the sumi outward. You now have two concentric rings.
Repeat: sumi at the centre, then dispersant at the centre. Each pair of drops adds another ring to the pattern. For multi-colour work, alternate different coloured inks in the same drop sequence.
When the rings are as dense as you want, breathe very gently across the surface from one side. See
: the rings will ripple and flow into organic wave patterns.
Lay the paper face-down on the surface in a single smooth motion. Hold for 10 to 15 seconds. Lift slowly from one corner.

items.symptom: The rings do not expand, ink just sits in a tight dot
items.cause: The ink is too viscous; or the dispersant was applied first before a sumi base was present.
items.fix: Always drop sumi first, then dispersant. Thin the sumi slightly with distilled water if it is not spreading at all.

items.symptom: Pattern smears when the paper is lifted
items.cause: Lifted too quickly or at too steep an angle.
items.fix: Lift slowly from one corner at a shallow angle, peeling the paper off the surface rather than pulling it up.
